_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: User Input
summary: Read keyboard input and check whether it matches the expected type.
refresher: Use `std::cin >> value` to read one value from the keyboard. The variable's type tells C++ what kind of input to expect. Use `std::getline` when you need a full line that may contain spaces.

blocks.body: Programs can pause and read what a user types. `std::cin >> age;` reads one value into `age`. Because `age` is an integer, C++ expects the user to enter a whole number.

The `>>` form normally stops at a space, which is useful for one number or one word. `std::getline(std::cin, name)` reads a complete line, including spaces. When you switch from `>>` to `getline`, use `std::ws` to skip the leftover newline.

blocks.title: Python input starts as text
blocks.body: Python's `input()` returns a string, and you convert it with something like `int(input())`. C++ can read directly into an `int`, but the read may fail when the typed text is not a whole number.

blocks.caption: The if condition is true only when C++ successfully reads a whole number.
Use std::ws before getline so the leftover Enter key does not become an empty name.

blocks.body: After the user types an age and presses Enter, `>>` reads the digits but can leave the newline behind. `std::ws` removes that waiting whitespace, so `getline` pauses for the person's name instead of returning an empty string.

blocks.title: Check whether conversion worked
blocks.body: If a user types `hello` when C++ expects an integer, the read fails. Test `std::cin` before using the number so invalid input follows a clear error path.

blocks.why: The input stream records a failure state that can be tested in a condition.

blocks.prompt: Which reads an entire line with spaces?
blocks.why: Getline continues through spaces until it reaches the line delimiter.
